description: You are acting as an on-call configuration-manager who must take a quick, point-in-time snapshot of the running system. Create ONE new log file whose exact path is   /home/user/config_audit/first_snapshot.log and make sure the parent directory (/home/user/config_audit) exists. You do NOT need elevated privileges. Gather four kinds of data (kernel version, top-memory processes, open listening ports, and filesystem usage) using any standard GNU/Linux utilities you like, but the final log file must respect the precise, machine-testable structure shown below: 1. The very first line is the literal word  TIMESTAMP: followed by a single space and the current date-and-time in the format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (24-hour clock). Example: TIMESTAMP: 2024-06-01 16:23:08 2. Immediately after that, the file contains four clearly delimited sections. Each section starts with a header line that is the section name enclosed in triple-equals signs, followed by exactly one newline, followed by the command output, followed by exactly one blank line. The required section names—and the order that must be respected—are: ===KERNEL=== ===TOP_PROCESSES=== ===LISTEN_PORTS=== ===DISK_USAGE=== That yields the following canonical skeleton (blank lines represented by “(blank)” for clarity only): TIMESTAMP: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS ===KERNEL=== (blank) ===TOP_PROCESSES=== (blank) ===LISTEN_PORTS=== (blank) ===DISK_USAGE=== (blank, but it is the last newline in the file) Important formatting rules the automated checker will enforce: • The section headers must appear exactly—case-sensitive—as shown above. • There must be a single blank line after each section’s data block, including the last one. • No extra spaces are allowed at the end of any header line. • The file must be world-readable (permissions 644) but writable only by you. Hints (not mandatory): • “uname -r” is sufficient for the KERNEL section. • “ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -n 6” gives five highest memory consumers plus header. • “ss -tuln” or “netstat -tuln” works for LISTEN_PORTS. • “df -h” is usually good for DISK_USAGE. When finished, verify the file layout with “cat -n /home/user/config_audit/first_snapshot.log”; the line numbers will help you ensure blank lines are in the right places. The automated tests will: a. Confirm the directory exists. b. Confirm the file exists and has mode 644. c. Parse the file to check the timestamp line, the presence and order of all four section headers, and that exactly one blank line follows each header’s content. If any part of the format differs, the task will be marked wrong. Do not output anything other than the required log file. All commands must be executed from the interactive shell. Good luck!
truth:
Directory that must exist before verification:
  /home/user/config_audit

File that must be created:
  /home/user/config_audit/first_snapshot.log

Permissions that will be checked:
  -rw-r--r-- (octal 0644)

Validation rules used by the hidden test harness (pseudo-code):

1) Read first line; assert it matches regex:  
   /^TIMESTAMP: \d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}$/

2) Read remainder; assert the following literal sequence of headers appears, each followed by at least one non-empty line containing command output, then a single completely blank line:
   ===KERNEL===  
   ===TOP_PROCESSES===  
   ===LISTEN_PORTS===  
   ===DISK_USAGE===

For each header H:
  a) There must be at least one line of non-whitespace data after H.  
  b) Exactly one blank line (^\s*$) must follow those data lines.

3) Ensure the very last line of the file is blank (terminating newline).

The harness does not attempt to validate the *content* of the command outputs (they are dynamic), only that they are non-empty.

No other files or directories are inspected.
